OUESTION
Are appel■ate court justices subject to the new gift and

CONCLUSION
Appellate court justiCes are not elected state officers′

consequently. this advice is subject to change.
1. Are Appellate Court Justices Elected State Officers?
As we dj-scussed in our telephone conversation, the Act now
provides restrictions on receipt of honoraria and gifts by state
and local elected officers, members of state boards and
commissions, and designated enployees of state agencj-es.
(Sections 89501, et seq. )
Section 895o2 provides:
(a) No elected state officer may accept an
honorar ium .
(b) rrHonorariumrr means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any palalent made in consideration
for any speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
conventj-on, meetingr social event, meaI, or Iike
gather ing .
***
(f) 
For purposes of this section, "efected
state officer" i-ncludes the Insurance commissioner.
The Act does not treat appellate court justices as elected
state officers. 
Section a2O21 of the Act defines "elected state
of f icerrr as any person who hol-ds an t'el-ective state of f ice, rr which
is defined in Section a2O24 to mean the office of covernor,
Lieutenant Governor, Aftorney censral, Controller, Secretary of
State. Treasurer, Superintendent of Public Instruction, member of
the Legislature and menber of the State Board of Equalization.
This definition does not include judicial officers. 
(Ohnan Advice
Letter, No. A-86-132; Snith Advice Letter, No. A-86-152, copies
enclosed. ) Consequentl-y, pursuant to the definition in Section
82O2t, appellate court justices are not considered elected state
officers who are subject to the gift or honoraria restrictions in
Section A95O2.3 /
3/  A further indication that appe■late court justices were not
intended to be subject to the restri9tions of sections 89502 and
89504 is the definitiOn of :le■
ected state officer‖
 set fOrth in
the Analysis of the Legislative Ana■
yst for senate Constitutional
Amendment 
■■2 which was presented to the voters in 
」une of 
■99o.
‖State officer as used in this section′
 means the Governor′
Lieutenant covernor′
 Attorney ceneral, controller′
 Insurance
Commissioner, secretary Of state′
 superintendent of Pub■
ic
lnstruction′  Treasurer and member of the board of equalization.::

2. Are Appeltate court Justices Members of a state Board or
Cornmiss ion?
Section 89503 extends sinitar prohibitions to a rtnenber of a
state board or commission.rt rrMember of a state board or
commissj-on" is not defined in the Act. However, section 82048
expressly includes menbers of decision-rnaking boards and
commissions in the definition of "public officiaI. 
rrhile expressly
excluding judges. Moreover, Regrulation 18700 (copy enclosed)
provides :
(a) t'Pubtic official at any 1evel of state or
loca1 gTovernmentrr means every natural person who is
a menber, officer, enployee, or consultant of a
state or 1oca1 governnent agency.
(1) 
rrMenberrr sha1l include, but not be
l-irnited to, salaried or unsalaried members of
boards or comnissions with decision-making
authority. 
A board or commission has
decision-making author j,ty srhenever 3
(A) It nay make a final
governmental decision;
(B) It rnay compel a governmental
decision; or it nay Prevent a
governmentaf decision either by reason of
an exclusive povrer to initiate the deci-
sion or by reason of a veto which may not
be overridden; or
(C) It makes substantive
recommendations which are, and over an
extended period of time have been,
regularfy approved without signif icant
amendment or modification by another
public official 
or governnental agency.
(Enphas j.s added. )
The descriptj.on in Regulation 1.8700 does not appear to
include judges. Since the Act does not treat judges as nembers of
boards or commissions, we do not believe the new ethics provisions
redefined the terms as used in the gift and honoraria sections of
the Act.


our File No. I -91- 104
Page 4
3. Are Appellate Court Justices Desiqnated Emplovees of a State
Aqencv?
Section 89503 also applies to a rrdesignated employee of a
state agency. " Appellate court justices are not designated
employees. section 8201,9 which defines the tern "designated
employee" provides in pertinent part:
I'Designated enployeerr does not include an
elected state officer, any unsalaried member of any
board or commission which serves a solely advisory
function, anv public official 
specified in Section
872oo I and al-so does not include any unsalaried
member of a nonregulatory conmittee, section,
cornrnission, or other such entity of the state Bar
of California.
(Emphasis added. )
Judges are expressly included in Section 872oo.
4. Are AppeIlate Court Justices LocaI Elected Officers?
The new provisions of the Act also place restrictions on the
ability of IocaI elected officers to receive gifts and honoraria.
Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shalI accept any
honoraria for any speech given, articl-e published,
or attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering, or any gifts, from any sing).e source,
which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000), in any qalendar year, exeept
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonabl-e subsistence in connection therewith.
The term rrlocal elected officeholderrr is not defined in the
Act. 
However, we can look to other areas of the Law for guidance.
A. other Provisions of the Act
section 82041 defines the term "loca1 government agencyrr as
follows:
[A] county. city or district 
of any kind
including school district, 
or any other local or
regional political 
subdivision, ,,or any departrnent,
division, bureau, office, board, cornmission or
other agency of the foregoing-
Emphasis added.
This section was amended in l-984 to broaden the scope of the
disclosure and disgual i fication provisions of the Act. brior to


Our File No. I-9■
―■04
Page 5
the amendment the courts were specifically excluded from the
definition. 
Thus. court personnel vere not subject to the
disclosure or d isqual i f ication provisions of the Act. At that
time, irowever, judges were already required to file statements of
econornic interests under Sectiorl 872OO, but were exempted from the
d isqual i f ication provisions of the Act.
The 1984 amendment included the courts in the definition of
I'Ioca1 government agency" to subject court personnel to the
disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act. 
However,
the Legisfature stated that the anendment of the secti-on was not
intended to affect the respons ibilities 
of judges. (LeS.
counsel's Dig. of Senate Bi]l l-427 (1984 Reg. sess.).) 
In fact
the bill also amended Section 82048 to provide an express
exemption from the d isqua I if ication requirements of the Act for
judges and court commissj,oners. Thus, currently, judges contj.nue
to discLose econornic interests under Section 872oo, but are
exempted from the conf l ict-of- interest provisions of the Act.
Another section that may provide guidance is Section 82o35.
section 82o35 defines the "jurisdictionrr of the various persons
who must discl-ose econonic interests under the Act. Under Section
82035, judges have always been considered state officials for
purposes of disclosure:
while for certain purposes the "jurisdiction 
rl
of a rnunicipal court judge nay extend only to the
county in which he sits, the nature of the judicial
system is such that a rnunicipal court's decision
can di-rectly affect persons or property throughout
the state.... 
In essence the court's jurisdiction
spreads statewide. In contrast, the decisions of
city or county efected officials general-Iy can
directly affect only those within the boundaries of
the city or county and will- not have a statewj-de
effect. 
(In re Batv (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 10. at 11-
12, copy enclosed. )
Consequently, judges disclose economic interests statewide.
In the area of canpaign regulation, appellate court justices
are required to file canpaign disclosure statenents at the same
locations as elected state officers. 
section 84215 requires:
IC]ampaign statenents shall be filed at the
following places:
(a) Statewide elected officers, candidates
for these offices, supreme court iusiices, their
controlled committees, cornmittees formed or
existing prinarily to support or oppose these


Our Fi■e No. I-9■―■04
Page 6
candidates, eLected officers, supreme court
justices, or statewide measures, or the
qualifications of state baLlot measures, and all
state general purpose com:nittees and filers not
specified in subdivisions (b) to (e), inclusive:
(1) The oriqinal and one copv with the
Secretarv of State.
(2) Two copies with the Registrar-
Recorder of Los Angeles county.
(3) Two copies with the Registrar of
voters of the city and county of San
Francisco.
(b) Members of the Legislature or Board of
Equalization, court of appeal iustices, superior
court judges, candj-dates for those offices, their
control]ed committees, and committees forrned or
existing prj-marily to support or oppose these
candidates or officeholders:
(1) The oriqinal and one copv vrith the
Secretary of State.
(21 Two copies $/ith the clerk of the
county with the largest nurnber of registered
voters in the districts affected.
Emphasis added.
Elected officers of local- districts. 
other than legislative
districts, 
Board of Equalization districts or appellate court
districts, 
county elected officers, rnunicipal court judgei, cj-ty
elected officers. candidates for city office are not required to
file forms with the secretary of state. 
(Section 84275(c) -(S).)
subdivision (c) applicable to eLected officers of districts 
which
cover more than a single county expressl-y excludes Iegislative
districts, 
Board of Equalization districts 
and appellate court
districts. 
(section 84215 (c) . )
Thus, the Act appears to treat the courts and court personnel
as local- public officials rrhile treating judges as state
officials.
B. Other Bodies of Law
This same dichotorny appears i., itter bodies of 1aw. The
courts are established and regulated by the state as set forth in
the constitution. 
(Cal. Const., art. VI , Section 3.) The state
Constitution provides :


our File No. I-9■
―■04
Page 7
The judicial power of this state shalf be
vested in the supreme court' courts of appeal,
superior courts, rnunicipal courts, and justice
courts.
(Art.icle vI, section 1. )
consistent with this constitutional provision, in 1980, the
supreme court concluded in olson v. corv (27 cal.3d 532, at 543)
that judges were state officers with respect to a constj-tutional
provilion that provided: "Salaries of elected state officers nay
not be reduced during their terrn of off ice.rr (cal. const. art. I,
Section l. ) 4/
Yet, in villanazul v. citv of Los Anqeles (1951) 37 caf.2d
7La, 722. the Supreme court concl,uded that despite the statev/ide
nature of the courts, deputy marshals attached to the courts were
county official-s for the purpose of the county's Iiability 
for
negJ-igence in the scope of the marshal's employment. Thus, while
there does appear to be a quasi-locat nature to the courts, judges
have been regarded as state elected officers.
consequentfy, in balance, it does not appear that appeIJ-ate
court justices are loca1 elected officeholders subject to the
Act's new gift or honoraria Iirnits.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns- Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free
to contact me at (91-6) 322-5901.
Sincerely′
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
